mnemonics.title: OS Roles: RESCUE
mnemonics.content: Remember: Resource manager, Environment provider, Service provider, Control unit, User interface, Efficiency optimizer - the key roles of an Operating System.

mnemonics.title: System Call Types: CREATE
mnemonics.content: Create, Read/Write, Execute, Allocate/Terminate, Update - mnemonic for common system call categories.

mnemonics.title: OS Components: PMMD
mnemonics.content: Process Management, Memory Management, Device Management - core components to remember.

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing process state and information
Interface for applications to interact with OS services
Unique ID assigned to each process

visualTips.visualization: Think of a bank vault: User mode is like being a customer in the lobby (limited access), while Kernel mode is like being the bank manager with a key to the vault (full access). The mode bit acts like an ID badge showing current privilege level.

Imagine a toll booth: User programs approach like cars wanting to cross a bridge. The system call is like stopping at the toll booth (trap), paying the fee (switching mode), then continuing on the other side (kernel execution).
Draw a horizontal line splitting a box: Bottom half is kernel space (privileged, direct hardware access), top half is user space (restricted, isolated). System calls cross this boundary.

analogies.analogy: Just as a government manages national resources (roads, hospitals, defense) and provides services (permits, security) to citizens, an OS manages hardware resources and provides services to applications.
A hotel manager allocates rooms (memory), manages staff (CPU time), handles guest requests (I/O), and ensures security (protection) - similar to OS resource management.
The kernel is like the brain stem controlling vital functions - it runs continuously, controls essential operations, and mediates between the body (hardware) and thoughts (applications).
